questions.question: What is the name of the girl sailing on the boat?
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly mentions ”Lizzie,” the man’s daughter, on the boat.

questions.question: Which of the following things are found on the boat?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions a fishing net and a coil of rope on the boat. There is no mention of a cushion, rusty boat-hook, or compass.

questions.question: Match the words in List I with their meanings in List II. LIST I LIST II 2 A. slime I. any unpleasant thick liquid substance B. bilme II. to be very afraid of something C. gaze III. extreme and forceful D. dread IV . to look steadily for a long time
questions.explanation: These word meanings are standard dictionary definitions.

questions.question: What is the relation between the two on the boat?
questions.explanation: The passage describes the man on the boat as recognizing his daughter.

questions.question: Choose the correct meaning of the word SODDEN.
questions.explanation: ”Sodden” means thoroughly saturated with liquid, especially water.

questions.question: Who adopted the pen name Premchand?
questions.explanation: The passage states that Dhanpat Rai adopted the pen name Premchand later in his writing career.

questions.question: According to the passage, Anandi is the heroine of:
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly names Anandi as the heroine of the novel *Godaan*.

questions.question: Match List I with List II to complete the information in the table. LIST I LIST II A. Dhanpat Rai I. Birth place of Premchand B. Nawab Rai II. Pen name used for short stories C. Premchand’s III. Premchand’s father D. Premchand’s IV . pen name used for short stories
questions.explanation: This requires careful matching of names and details given in the passage.

questions.question: One of Premchand’s short stories is about a character from real life, named Kazmi who was a:
questions.explanation: The text clearly identifies Kazmi as a postal messenger.

questions.question: According to the passage, who is responsible for Premchand learning Urdu and Persian?
questions.explanation: The passage mentions that Premchand learned Urdu and Persian during his early 6 schooling in Lamhia, this implicates Yahyaand.

questions.question: Match List I with List II to complete the information given in the table: List I List II A. Persuasive Speech i. transfers knowledge B. Entertaining Speech ii. have a touch of reality C. Informative Speech iii. changes mindsets D. Anecdote iv. makes you feel good
questions.explanation: A persuasive speech aims to change mindsets (III). An entertaining speech makes you feel good (IV). An informative speech transfers knowledge (I). An anecdote has a touch of reality (II).

questions.question: The elements of an Entertaining Speech are - A. It is not bound by time, it can go on forever. B. It needs a good opening with an anecdotes C. It should be a third person account D. It is effective when presented in the first person account and in the present tense E. It is knowledge based.
questions.explanation: An entertaining speech benefits from a strong opening, often using anecdotes (B), and is more effective when delivered in the first person and present tense for immediacy and engagement (D).

questions.question: ”An entertaining speech sells or promotes happiness and GRATITUDE. Replace the underlined word with its synonym.”
questions.explanation: Gratitude means thankfulness or appreciation.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct option to state one of the features of the Entertaining Speech. While presenting an Entertaining Speech, should be taken care of for effective outreach.
questions.explanation: Pace and modulation (variation in speed and tone) are crucial for keeping an entertaining speech engaging and effective.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of an Entertaining Speech?
options: The Best Man’s speech at a wedding reception
questions.explanation: A valedictory at a seminar is typically informative or persuasive, focusing on summarizing key takeaways and thanking participants, rather than primarily entertaining.

questions.question: Choose the correct Passive form of the sentence: ”This book will change your life.”
options: Book would change you and life.
Your life will be changed by this book.
Life is going to be changed in the book.
questions.explanation: The correct passive voice construction is ”Your life will be changed by this book,” 10 maintaining the future tense and correctly shifting the object (”your life”) to the subject position.

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives suggested, select the one which best expresses the following in Passive Voice: They are teasing you.
options: They were being teased by you.
They are being teased by you.
They have been teasing by you.
They are being teased by you.
questions.explanation: The correct passive voice is ”They are being teased by you,” retaining the present continuous tense and making ”you” the agent of the action.

questions.question: Out of the given options, choose the Direct Speech for the sentence: He exclaimed sadly that he was undone.
questions.explanation: The direct speech captures the emotion (”Alas!”) and the original wording (”I am 11 undone”) conveyed in the indirect statement.

questions.question: Pick the correct Indirect Speech for the given sentence: ”Rakesh said to Mahesh, ’Go away’.”
options: Rakesh asked Mahesh to go away.
Rakesh ordered Mahesh to go away.
Rakesh wanted Mahesh to go away.
questions.explanation: “Rakesh ordered Mahesh to go away” best reflects the imperative nature of the original sentence, where Rakesh is commanding Mahesh to leave.

questions.question: Rearrange the phrases to make a meaningful sentence: A. type of my heart could soften B. in some objects including human tissue C. penetrate that Roentgen D. discovered a new
questions.explanation: The correct order forms the sentence: ”Roentgen discovered a new type of heart that could soften and penetrate some objects including human tissue.

questions.question: Rearrange the given phrases to form a sentence with superlative degree of comparison: of all the metals / iron / is the most useful.
options: Iron of all the metals is the most useful.
Iron is the most useful of all the metals.
Of all the metals most useful is iron.
Iron is one of all metals the most useful.
questions.explanation: The correct arrangement is ”Iron is the most useful of all the metals,” which adheres to standard superlative adjective construction (”the most useful”) and sentence structure.

questions.question: The phrase ”VIVE LA FRANCE” means:
questions.explanation: ”Vive la France” is a French expression meaning ”Long live France,” expressing patriotism and support for the country.

questions.question: Choose the correct meaning of the expression ’TOUR DE FORCE’:
questions.explanation: A ”tour de force” refers to a feat of skill, strength, or ingenuity, often in artistic or creative endeavors.

questions.question: Choose the correct option that can replace the word ’CHAUVINIST’:
options: a person who is blindly devoted to an idea
a person appointed by his partners to solve his oratory
a leader who always betrays followers by his oratory
a person who is unable to pay his debts
questions.explanation: A chauvinist is someone who has excessive or prejudiced loyalty or support for their own cause, group, or sex.

questions.question: Match the words in List I with their meanings in List II List I List II A. Oracle I. A person skilled in foreign languages B. Pundit II. one who can be fooled easily C. Impostor III. The advice given by Gods D. Gullible IV . a learned man
questions.explanation: An oracle provides advice from gods (III). A pundit is a learned person (IV). An impostor is someone skilled in foreign languages (often used deceptively) (I). A gullible person is easily fooled (II).

questions.question: Match the blanks in List I with suitable Articles in List II List I List II A. BBC is starting a new campaign. I. No article B. Everyone was born by definition. II. an C. The COVID Pandemic is universal problem. III. a D. heart of the UNESCO heritage site. IV . the
questions.explanation: ”The” is used before specific entities like the BBC (A-IV). ”No article” is used with abstract nouns like ’definition’ after ’by’ (B-I). ”A” is used before a singular, countable noun like ’universal problem’ (C-III), and “The” is used with specific nouns, like “heart” when referring to a particular location (D-II)

questions.question: Choose appropriate option to complete the following sentence: Vikram wrote letter to his university yesterday but he forgot to post it.
questions.explanation: ”A” is the correct indefinite article for a singular, countable noun (”letter”) that is not yet specific. Since it is not referring to any particular letter, ”a” is used. Understanding Articles: ”A” is used before words starting with consonant sounds, and it indicates something indefinite or non-specific.

questions.question: Convert the following sentence into its Exclamatory form: ”I wish I were young again!”
options: Hurrah! I could be young again!
Oh! that I were young again!
questions.explanation: “Oh! that I were young again!” correctly expresses the wish in an exclamatory manner. The exclamatory form is used to show strong emotion or feeling. Exclamatory Sentences: These sentences often begin with words like ”Oh!”, ”Wow!”, or ”Alas!” and express strong feelings, especially desires or regrets.

questions.question: What is delicious is an Exclamatory sentence for which of the following Assertive Sentences?
options: It was what is delicious meal.
It is a meal very delicious.
questions.explanation: ”This was a delicious meal” is the assertive sentence corresponding to the exclamatory form ”What is delicious!” Understanding Assertive vs. Exclamatory Sentences: Assertive sentences provide information or statements, whereas exclamatory sentences express strong feelings or surprise.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the correct Preposition: The gift was so beautiful that he was overwhelmed joy.
questions.explanation: ”Overwhelmed with joy” is the correct idiomatic expression used to show that someone is overcome by a particular emotion. Prepositions with Idioms: Prepositions like ”with” often appear in set phrases, and ”overwhelmed with” is one of those fixed expressions used to describe an emotional reaction.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: The lion and the unicorn fought for the crown.
Humpty Dumpty sat on a wall.
Here is the watch that you asked for.
There is a cattle into the field.
questions.explanation: ”Cattle” is a plural noun; therefore, the sentence should be ”There are cattle in the field” rather than ”There is a cattle.” Context of Plural Nouns: ”Cattle” is always treated as a plural noun, and it does not take the singular article ”a.” When referring to cattle, use ”are” instead of ”is.”

questions.question: Fill in the blank with appropriate Modal: You obey your parents.
questions.explanation: ”Must” conveys the strongest obligation or necessity to obey one’s parents, compared to ”should” or ”ought to,” which are less forceful. Understanding Modals: Modals like ”must,” ”should,” and ”ought to” express different degrees of obligation or permission. ”Must” expresses the highest degree of necessity.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate option to complete the following statement: ”After the meeting concluded, I give my side of the story.”
questions.explanation: ”Was allowed to” is the correct verb form, indicating past tense and passive voice (I was given permission to speak). Using Passive Voice: Passive voice emphasizes the action done to the subject, often focusing on the permission received. 19

questions.question: The first thing to be written while writing a formal letter is
questions.explanation: In a formal letter, the sender’s address is typically placed first, followed by the date, recipient’s address, salutation, and then the body of the letter. Letter Format: Formal letters follow a structured format, and placing the sender’s address at the top ensures clarity in identifying the source of the letter.

questions.question: Which amongst the following is NOT part of an informal letter?
questions.explanation: An invoice is a formal document used for billing and payment, not a component of an informal letter. Informal Letter Structure: Informal letters are used for personal communication and are less structured compared to formal letters. They omit formal elements like invoices. 20

questions.question: Which amongst the following options carries the correct spelling?
questions.explanation: The correct spelling is ”Phenomenology,” a philosophical study of experience and consciousness.

questions.question: Identify the incorrect example of Past Perfect Tense from the given options:
options: I had gone to the gymnasium by the time you arrived.
I had just settled into bed when the guests arrived.
I had stepped into my office when the phone rang.
questions.explanation: ”I had stepped into my office when the phone rang” is in the past simple form when the phone rang, implying the call interrupted the action. Past perfect requires the first completed action and the second action to happen after that in past simple. The correct form should be ”I had stepped into the office when the phone rang.” 21

questions.question: Match the Phrasal Verbs in List I with their meanings in List II List I List II A. stand up for I. removed B. strike off II. become possible C. turn up III. support D. turn against IV . oppose
questions.explanation: ”Stand up for” means to support (A-III). ”Strike off” means to remove (B-I). ”Turn up” means to become possible or to arrive (C-II). ”Turn against” means to oppose (D-IV).

questions.question: Choose the sentence in which the Phrasal Verb use of ’Polish off’ has been used correctly:
options: The servant polished off the shoes of all inmates.
My brother polished off the cake that was in the box.
The Speaker polished off his speech beautifully.
All the students polished off their text books completely.
questions.explanation: ”Polished off” means to finish quickly or completely. It’s correctly used in option 2, where the brother quickly finished the cake.

questions.question: Match the words in List I with their meanings in List II List I List II A. Cite I. to quote words from a book B. Petrol II. place where a building is situated C. Site III. Fuel is liquid used for vehicles D. Bite IV . to go around checking for safety
questions.explanation: ”Cite” means to quote (I). ”Petrol” is fuel (III). ”Site” refers to a location (II). ”Bite” could refer to going around checking for safety (in the sense of a police dog, for instance). (IV).

questions.question: Rearrange the jumbled words to form a meaningful sentence: A. displacements from B. further lens C. large-scale 23 D. special E. economic zones
questions.explanation: The correct order forms the sentence: ”Large-scale displacements from special economic zones further lens.” While the sentence might sound a bit unusual, it implies that mass displacements due to economic zones are being observed or analyzed (using ”lens” metaphorically to represent focus or perspective). Grammatical Structure: The phrase “further lens” is likely a metaphor suggesting that these displacements provide additional insight or perspective on economic zones, even though the wording seems unconventional.

questions.question: Choose the word that is opposite in meaning to ”SPURIOUS”:
questions.explanation: ”Spurious” refers to something false, counterfeit, or lacking authenticity. The antonym of spurious is ”genuine,” meaning authentic or real. Context: Understanding opposites like ”spurious” (false) and ”genuine” (real) helps in enhancing vocabulary and distinguishing between different types of objects or information. 24

questions.question: The food served for lunch was INSIPID. Rewrite the sentence by giving the antonym to the underlined word.
questions.explanation: ”Insipid” means lacking flavor, dull, or unappetizing. The appropriate antonym is ”delicious,” which describes food that has a pleasing and enjoyable taste. Context: ”Insipid” is often used to describe bland or boring food, while ”delicious” is used to highlight something flavorful and enjoyable.

questions.question: Match the blanks in List I with the correct Adverbs in List II List I List II A. He will pay for his mistake. I. ever B. I do not remember having stories before. II. dearly C. I think that Ravi succeeded in scoring a century III. scarcely D. does anyone believe in ghost these days. IV . barely
questions.explanation: The correct matching is: • A. He will pay ”dearly” (a high price) for his mistake (A-II). • B. ”Scarcely” means barely or hardly (B-III). • C. Ravi succeeded ”barely” (C-IV), implying that his success was not easily achieved. • D. ”Barely” anyone believes in ghosts these days (D-IV), emphasizing the rarity of belief. Context of Adverbs: Adverbs such as ”barely” and ”scarcely” express limitations or rarity, whereas ”dearly” emphasizes the intensity or value of an action.

questions.question: Sanjeev stayed at work he could complete the pending work.
questions.explanation: ”So that” is used to indicate purpose. In this sentence, it shows the reason for Sanjeev staying at work — to complete his pending work. Context of ”So That”: ”So that” is commonly used to explain the purpose or result of an action, such as working to achieve a goal. 26

questions.question: Out of the four alternatives, choose the one that suits the best as a question tag for the sentence: She rarely attends the class, ?
questions.explanation: The correct question tag is ”does she?” Question tags use the auxiliary verb (or ”do/does/did” if no auxiliary is present) and the pronoun from the main clause, and they are opposite in polarity (positive sentence, negative tag; negative sentence, positive tag). Since the main clause is negative in meaning (”rarely attends”), the tag is positive (”does she”). Context of Question Tags: Question tags are used to confirm or check information. When the sentence is negative, the tag is positive, and vice versa.

questions.question: Rearrange the words in the correct order to form a meaningful sentence: A. ever B. happens C. by D. chance E. nothing
questions.explanation: The correct sentence is ”Nothing ever happens by chance,” meaning that events are not random or accidental. Grammar Tip: In English, the word order is crucial to making sentences meaningful. Adverbs like ”ever” typically come before the verb, and prepositional phrases like ”by chance” usually follow the verb.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the phrase ’A feather in one’s cap’?
options: something that you have achieved and are proud of
something that is fragile and thus important to you
something that exists in colours and makes you stand out.
something that makes you feel soft and delicate.
questions.explanation: ”A feather in one’s cap” is an idiom meaning an achievement that one is proud of, something that adds to one’s prestige or reputation. Context of Idioms: The phrase ”a feather in one’s cap” comes from the practice of warriors adding a feather to their cap as a symbol of their victories.

questions.question: Read the text below and identify the options that shows the use of an idiomatic expression: 28 I went to stay with my cousin last month. We are of the same age but have very little in common. He loves sports and I hate it.
questions.explanation: ”Have very little in common” is an idiom meaning to share few interests or characteristics. Context of Idioms: Idioms often express abstract ideas in more concrete terms. In this case, ”have very little in common” conveys the idea of minimal shared interests.

questions.question: Choose the option that serves as an Adjective Clause in the sentence: The boy, whom you see bowling, is my cousin.
questions.explanation: ”Whom you see bowling” is the adjective clause, modifying the noun ”boy” by providing more information about him. Context of Adjective Clauses: Adjective clauses provide more detail about a noun or pronoun in a sentence. They usually begin with a relative pronoun (who, whom, whose, that, which). 29
